objective: Use these strategy categories to invent net-new Atlas baremetal tests that close measured RTL coverage gaps, strengthen subsystem integration coverage, reflect realistic Pi0-style accelerator workloads, and avoid redundant regressions.

strategy_categories.name: Uncovered Integration Frontiers
strategy_categories.description: Choose gaps where multiple Atlas subsystems should interact but currently do not appear together in one coherent dataflow.
strategies: Target subsystem combinations that the analyzer explicitly marks as absent or shallowly covered.
Prefer tests that exercise at least one data-movement stage, one compute stage, and one control or validation stage in the same program.
Prioritize combinations that force realistic producer-consumer flows such as DMA -> VMEM -> VPU/MXU -> DMA store-back.
Use both MXU0 and MXU1 when concurrency or dual-pipeline behavior is a meaningful differentiator.
Favor dataflows where the output of one functional unit becomes the direct input of another, such as DMA -> VLOAD -> VPU transform -> MXU consume -> VSTORE/DMA.

strategy_categories.name: Pi0 Workload Grounding
strategy_categories.description: Bias ideas toward the kinds of end-to-end tensor movement and compute patterns Atlas is meant to accelerate for the Pi0 model, not toy opcode demos.
strategies: Prefer tests that look like plausible Pi0 kernels or subgraphs: staged tensor loads, vector transforms, matrix-heavy compute, reductions, requantization, and store-back.
Use operand sizes, tiling plans, and producer-consumer sequencing that resemble real accelerator work rather than isolated single-instruction smoke tests.
When choosing between equally valid ideas, select the one that better resembles a realistic Pi0 inference pipeline with multiple dependent stages.
Include control, synchronization, and memory-management behavior that would naturally arise in a model-serving workload, not just arithmetic in isolation.

strategy_categories.name: Functional Unit Chaining And Overlap
strategy_categories.description: Deliberately exercise handoff, overlap, and backpressure between Atlas engines so the planner covers execution fabric behavior instead of only isolated unit correctness.
strategies: Exhaust meaningful opportunities to overlap independent work across DMA, LSU, MXU, VPU, XLU, and scalar control without violating documented hazard rules.
Build tests where one engine prepares data while another consumes prior results, so overlap is part of the intended execution story rather than accidental timing noise.
Use all functional units across the generated portfolio, and prefer individual tests that activate more than one compute path when the coverage report suggests weak integration.
Stress explicit handoff paths such as XLU transpose feeding MXU, VPU pack/unpack feeding DMA traffic, or reduction results steering later control flow.

strategy_categories.name: Oracle And Check Design
strategy_categories.description: Decide how the test will prove correctness in a way that is debuggable and proportionate to the complexity of the computation.
strategies: Use assembly-only self-checking tests when the expected result is compact and can be derived inline with scalar instructions.
Use a golden generator when the test stores DRAM results, depends on randomized payloads, or combines enough stages that inline expected values would be brittle.
Make the failure mode localizable by checking intermediate milestones or by structuring results so mismatches point to a subsystem boundary.
Keep generators simple, deterministic, and tightly coupled to the assembly dataflow.

strategy_categories.name: Stress Dimensions
strategy_categories.description: Increase the chance of uncovering scheduler, hazard, alignment, and pipeline issues without making the test impossible to reason about.
strategies: Vary transfer sizes, address offsets, loop trip counts, and operand patterns to stress edge cases rather than only nominal cases.
Exercise non-trivial control flow such as loops, branches, or phased pipelines when it reveals integration behavior.
Use delay-sensitive instruction sequences where the required latency management is part of what the test is validating.
Favor medium-complexity tests that are still inspectable over giant kitchen-sink programs.
Make some tests intentionally pressure scoreboard and replacement behavior by forcing register reuse, VMEM buffer turnover, and staged result consumption.

strategy_categories.name: Memory And Layout Choices
strategy_categories.description: Shape VMEM and DRAM usage so the generated test models realistic movement of tensors and exposes address-generation mistakes.
strategies: Stage data through VMEM regions that reflect true producer-consumer handoff instead of artificial single-buffer shortcuts.
Choose layouts that require explicit address arithmetic, alignment discipline, and careful buffer reuse.
Use separate DRAM source and destination regions so the expected dataflow is auditable.
When practical, design the memory plan so one can reason about correctness from offsets and tile boundaries alone.
Include cases where VMEM is effectively full or heavily partitioned so the test must recycle buffers, replace tiles, or carefully avoid clobbering live data.

strategy_categories.name: Register And Capacity Pressure
strategy_categories.description: Create pressure on TRF, VMEM, and staging resources so the planner covers state replacement and long dependency chains rather than only clean-room setups.
strategies: Include tests that intentionally fill most or all relevant tensor-register slots before introducing the next phase of work.
Force replacement behavior by reusing registers and VMEM regions after their prior contents have been consumed, then verify that the new values are the ones that propagate.
Prefer multi-phase programs where live state must be managed carefully across loads, compute, stores, and follow-on compute.
Use capacity pressure to expose bugs in bank selection, stale-data hazards, and producer-consumer bookkeeping.

strategy_categories.name: Coverage Portfolio Balance
strategy_categories.description: Choose tests that improve weak or underrepresented areas in the measured coverage report and suite-level mix.
strategies: Bias new tests toward subsystems or subsystem pairings that remain underrepresented after accounting for the current suite and measured coverage report.
Prefer tests that connect isolated unit-style behaviors into stronger end-to-end coverage narratives.
Reduce blind spots around branch-delay handling, DMA synchronization, VMEM reuse, mixed compute pipelines, and functional-unit handoff behavior.
If multiple candidate tests are similar, pick the one most likely to complement the suite cleanly, activate a different set of engines, and raise overall confidence.
